g.data-narration: Welcome to Servlet Background. We'll explore the evolution from static web pages to dynamic Java Servlets.
g.text: Evolution from CGI to Java Servlets

g.data-narration: Understanding why servlets were developed helps us appreciate their architecture and design decisions.
The web evolved from static pages to dynamic content through three major phases: static, CGI, and servlets.
g.text: Each phase solved limitations of the previous technology

g.data-narration: In the early web, servers simply returned static HTML files. No processing or personalization was possible.
g.text: Server locates and returns pre-written HTML files - no customization possible

g.data-narration: CGI (Common Gateway Interface) was the first standard for generating dynamic content by executing external programs.
g.text: Each request spawns a NEW OS process - expensive overhead

g.data-narration: CGI had major problems: slow performance due to process creation, poor scalability, and no built-in session management.
Java Servlets solved all CGI limitations by running within a JVM and using threads instead of processes.
g.text: Sun Microsystems developed Servlets (1997) to address CGI limitations:

g.data-narration: A servlet is a Java class extending HttpServlet that handles HTTP requests and generates dynamic responses.
The servlet container manages servlet lifecycle, request routing, threading, security, and session management.
Servlets outperform CGI with thread-based execution, shared memory, persistence, and built-in session management.
g.text: New Process | Slow | No Sessions | Poor Scalability
Servlets reuse JVM threads, share memory, and persist between requests

g.data-narration: Servlets process HTTP requests through doGet, doPost, doPut, doDelete methods based on the request type.
Each HTTP method maps to a specific servlet method: GET to doGet(), POST to doPost(), etc.
The web.xml file configures servlets and their URL mappings for the web application deployment.
Web applications follow a standard directory structure with WEB-INF containing configuration and classes.
